5 min read
Just now

--

How a legitimate ad-tech tool became one of the most useful pieces of infrastructure in a threat actor’s toolkit.
Most write-ups on malware campaigns jump straight to the payload: the exploit kit, the phishing page, the ransomware binary. But there’s often a layer sitting in front of all that, quietly deciding who even gets to see it. That layer is a Traffic Distribution System, or TDS, and understanding it explains a lot about why some campaigns run for months without getting caught, while researchers staring at the same URL sometimes see nothing but a blank page or a harmless redirect.
What a TDS Is Supposed to Do
TDS platforms were built for advertisers and affiliate marketers. If you’re running an ad campaign across ten countries, you don’t want every click landing on the same generic page. You want a system that looks at where the click came from, what device it’s on, what language the browser is set to, and then routes that visitor to the most relevant landing page. Products like Keitaro were built exactly for this, and they’re still widely used for entirely legitimate purposes today.
The problem is that “route this visitor somewhere different based on who they are” is an extremely useful capability if what you’re routing people toward is malicious.
How Threat Actors Repurpose It
